name: New Era Energy & Digital, Inc.

profile.description: New Era Energy & Digital, Inc. develops and operates next-generation digital infrastructure and integrated power assets for AI hyperscalers. The company's flagship Texas Critical Data Centers project covers 438 acres in Ector County, Texas, targeting over 1 GW of compute capacity. Formerly known as New Era Helium, the company pivoted to AI data center infrastructure in August 2025.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (0/3): New Era Energy & Digital has no meaningful product launches or operational assets. The company was originally 'New Era Helium,' an oil-and-gas entity that renamed itself in August 2025 to ride the AI infrastructure narrative [1]. Total revenue was $802K in Q1 2026 — essentially zero for a company valued at $600M. No data center has been built, no compute capacity delivered, and no power infrastructure is operational. The pivot from helium extraction to AI data centers represents a name change and investor pitch, not a demonstrated track record of innovation. AI Deployment (0/2): Despite positioning itself as AI infrastructure, the company has zero operational AI products, services, or deployed data center capacity. The Texas Critical Data Centers project is entirely pre-construction, with power delivery targeted for the earliest at end of 2027 [1]. Market Share (0/2): New Era holds zero market share in any relevant category — data center infrastructure, power generation, or AI compute. The company is pre-operational and competes conceptually against hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon), established data center REITs (Equinix, Digital Realty), and well-capitalized infrastructure developers. Pipeline (1/2): The TCDC project has specific parameters — 438 acres in Ector County, Texas, targeting 1+ GW compute capacity — and the company claims to be in negotiations with a leading hyperscaler for a triple-net lease [2]. Full ownership of TCDC was secured after acquiring Sharon AI's 50% stake [1]. However, execution risk is extreme: no construction has begun, no power purchase agreements are finalized, and the timeline (end of 2027 for power delivery) is aspirational for a company with 5 employees and $802K in quarterly revenue. R&D Intensity (0/1): No R&D expenditures. The company has 5 employees and no technical development capabilities.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (1/4): New Era's stated mission as a 'developer and operator of next-generation digital infrastructure' is entirely generic — interchangeable with dozens of AI infrastructure SPACs and blank-check pivots [2]. The language does not articulate a distinctive purpose, identify who the company serves beyond profit motive, or describe why it exists beyond capturing the AI data center trend. Community Impact (0/2): No evidence of charitable giving, community programs, or any social investment. The company's predecessor entity, operating as New Era Helium and through subsidiary Solis Partners, is alleged by the New Mexico Attorney General to have left environmental remediation obligations unfulfilled through strategic bankruptcies and shell entities [3]. This is the opposite of community impact. Employee Retention (0/2): With only 5 employees, there is no meaningful organizational culture to evaluate. The rapid addition of C-suite titles (Chief Development Officer, General Counsel, Chief Accounting Officer, Chief Corporate Officer) in 2026 suggests a management team being assembled to manage capital, not a cohesive organization with shared values [2]. Insider Buying (0/2): Net insider selling — CEO Byron Roth and CFO Gordon Roth sold shares at $9.88 in December 2024. The 10%+ holder CR Financial also sold at $9.88 [4]. No insider buying was identified. Insiders selling at nearly the all-time high while promoting an aspirational project is a significant negative signal.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics, though nearly all financial data is distorted by the company's pre-operational status and tiny revenue base. D/E of 520.2% is extreme — far above the 300% floor, scoring 0 [4]. The $52.5M in total debt against $2.2M cash (though the company reports $80M+ cash as of April 2026 after equity raises and the $290M Macquarie credit facility [2]) reflects a highly leveraged, capital-intensive development stage. ROE of -959.2% reflects massive losses on minimal equity — scores 0 [4]. Revenue growth YoY of 145.8% and 5-year CAGR of 20.2% both exceed the 10% target and score maximum, but these metrics are profoundly misleading: the base is $533K growing to $802K in quarterly revenue [1]. A lemonade stand could show similar percentage growth. Profit margin of 0% (net loss of $8.99M on $802K revenue) scores 0 [1]. Forward P/E and PEG are excluded due to negative/null values. The mechanical score of 4 overstates financial quality because percentage-based revenue growth metrics are meaningless on a sub-$1M revenue base.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): New Era's business model — building physical data center infrastructure for AI hyperscalers — is conceptually well-positioned as an AI enabler rather than an AI victim. Data center infrastructure is capital-intensive, location-dependent, and requires physical power delivery, creating structural barriers that AI software cannot displace [2]. If the TCDC project becomes operational, the company would serve the most voracious consumers of compute infrastructure (hyperscalers running large language models, training clusters, and inference workloads). The triple-net lease model under negotiation would make New Era essentially a real estate and power provider for AI compute. However, the protection score reflects the business model concept, not operational reality — the company has zero operational data center capacity, zero AI-related revenue, and faces extreme execution risk. The competitive moat is entirely theoretical: established players like Equinix, Digital Realty, QTS, and well-capitalized hyperscalers building their own capacity have massive advantages in capital, expertise, and customer relationships. AI Opportunity (0/1): No AI revenue generated. No operational infrastructure. No demonstrated ability to capture AI demand. The opportunity exists only on paper and in investor presentations.

infinite_game.summary: New Era Energy & Digital exhibits a textbook finite game posture — a company rebranding to capture a trending narrative (AI data centers), with insiders selling at highs, leadership drawn from financial engineering backgrounds rather than mission-driven infrastructure, and active securities fraud litigation alleging misleading statements about the company's core project [3][5]. The pivot from helium to AI infrastructure, while dramatic, appears driven by capital markets opportunity rather than a Just Cause. The 5-employee organization has no culture, no teams, and no track record of long-term thinking.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (0/3): New Era has no articulated purpose beyond profit and capital appreciation. The company's stated mission is generic ('next-generation digital infrastructure'), and its actions — pivoting from helium extraction to AI data centers in response to market trends, insiders selling shares at highs, and CEO Gray's background in oil-and-gas financial engineering — suggest the organization exists to capture investor capital around a trending narrative [2][3]. The New Mexico AG lawsuit alleging that CEO Gray formed affiliated entities to channel revenue while leaving environmental obligations with insolvent entities represents the antithesis of a Just Cause [3]. No evidence of purpose-driven leadership, stakeholder commitment, or long-term vision was identified.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): With 5 employees, there is no organizational structure capable of generating trusting teams. More critically, the federal securities fraud class action and New Mexico AG lawsuit alleging CEO-directed fraud through shell entities and strategic bankruptcies suggest a governance environment built on opacity rather than trust [3][5]. The rapid appointment of multiple C-suite officers in 2026 (Chief Development Officer, General Counsel, Chief Accounting Officer, Chief Corporate Officer) may represent infrastructure-building, but may also reflect the need for professional management to navigate legal exposure [2].

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): No evidence that New Era acknowledges or learns from competitors. The company does not publicly benchmark against established data center developers (Equinix, Digital Realty, QTS) or well-capitalized AI infrastructure competitors. Management communications focus on the TCDC project's potential rather than competitive positioning or what the company can learn from better-resourced rivals [2].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The pivot from New Era Helium to New Era Energy & Digital represents a dramatic strategic shift — abandoning helium extraction to pursue AI data center infrastructure [1][2]. This demonstrates willingness to reinvent the company in response to changing opportunities. However, the flexibility appears driven by financial opportunism (AI is the hottest capital markets narrative) rather than a Just Cause, and the company's ability to execute the pivot is entirely unproven. The acquisition of Sharon AI's 50% TCDC stake and the Macquarie $290M credit facility show commitment to the new direction, but commitment to a trend is not the same as existential flexibility in service of a purpose.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): No evidence of courageous long-term decisions. CEO Will Gray's background is oil-and-gas financial engineering (VP at Resaca Exploitation, CEO of Cross Border Resources, CEO of Dala Petroleum), and the New Mexico AG lawsuit alleges a pattern of forming entities, extracting value, and abandoning obligations through strategic bankruptcy [3]. Insider selling at $9.88 — near the all-time high — while promoting an aspirational project to retail investors is the opposite of courageous leadership [4]. The company has not made difficult short-term sacrifices for long-term stakeholder benefit.

investment_thesis: New Era Energy & Digital trades at a $600M market cap on $802K in quarterly revenue — a valuation disconnected from any operational reality [4]. The company's sole asset is the Texas Critical Data Centers project: 438 acres in Ector County, Texas, targeting 1+ GW of AI compute capacity with power delivery targeted for end of 2027 at the earliest [1]. The bull case rests entirely on the TCDC project becoming operational and securing hyperscaler leases: the company has full project ownership after acquiring Sharon AI's 50% stake, claims to be in negotiations with a leading hyperscaler for a triple-net lease, and has access to significant capital ($80M+ cash, up to $270M remaining on the Macquarie credit facility) [2]. Revenue growth of 146% YoY, while from a negligible base, shows some commercial activity.
thesis_continued: The bear case is overwhelming. A federal securities fraud class action alleges that New Era made false and misleading statements about the TCDC project, including permitting status, environmental liabilities, and related-party transactions [5]. The New Mexico Attorney General has sued CEO Will Gray, subsidiary Solis Partners, and the company for an alleged fraudulent oil-and-gas scheme involving self-dealing, shell entities, and strategic bankruptcies designed to evade well-plugging and environmental remediation obligations [3]. Insiders — CEO Byron Roth, CFO Gordon Roth, and 10%+ holder CR Financial — sold shares near the all-time high of $9.88 in December 2024 [4]. The company has 5 employees, $52.5M in debt, D/E of 520%, ROE of -959%, and a $600M valuation on $1.36M in annual revenue [1][4]. No data center has been built, no power infrastructure is operational, and the company is competing against hyperscalers and established data center operators with vastly more capital, expertise, and customer relationships. The combination of securities fraud litigation, AG enforcement action, insider selling, and pre-operational status makes NUAI uninvestable at current valuations.
key_catalysts: Execution of a binding triple-net lease with a major hyperscaler for TCDC would provide revenue visibility and validate the project — but no signed agreement exists, and negotiations could extend indefinitely or collapse [2]

TCDC power delivery commencing by end of 2027 would transform the company from a concept stock to an operational data center, though this timeline is aspirational for a 5-employee company with no construction underway [1]

Favorable resolution of the federal securities fraud class action and New Mexico AG lawsuit would remove the overhang, though litigation outcomes are highly uncertain and discovery could reveal additional liabilities [3][5]
key_risks: Federal securities fraud class action and New Mexico AG lawsuit alleging fraudulent schemes, self-dealing, and misleading statements create existential legal risk — adverse outcomes could result in injunctions, disgorgement, penalties, or management removal that would destroy shareholder value [3][5]

Pre-operational company with $600M valuation on $1.36M annual revenue faces extreme execution risk — no data center constructed, no power infrastructure operational, no binding hyperscaler agreement, 5 employees, and competition from Equinix, Digital Realty, and hyperscaler self-builds with 1000x more resources [1][2][4]

Insider selling at $9.88 near the all-time high (CEO Roth, CFO Roth, CR Financial in December 2024) combined with D/E of 520%, ROE of -959%, and potential for further dilution from the $115M registered offering suggests insiders are not aligned with retail shareholders [4]

New Era Energy & Digital CEO Will Gray — leadership team expansion, Macquarie $290M credit facility, hyperscaler negotiations, 2025-2026
New Mexico Attorney General lawsuit against New Era, Solis Partners, and CEO Gray — fraudulent oil-and-gas scheme, self-dealing, strategic bankruptcies, December 2025
Yahoo Finance / StockAnalysis — NUAI key statistics, $5.92 stock price, $600M market cap, D/E 520%, accessed June 2026
